title: 10 best herbal teas for pertussis
intro_desc: Herbal teas for pertussis are infusions made from various dried herbs that can help alleviate symptoms of pertussis, also known as whooping cough. These teas can soothe a sore throat, reduce inflammation, and alleviate coughing spells. Examples of herbal teas that can be beneficial for pertussis include licorice root, thyme, and chamomile. Licorice root can help reduce inflammation and soothe a sore throat, while thyme has antimicrobial properties that can help fight off the bacteria that cause pertussis. Chamomile, on the other hand, has calming properties that can help promote relaxation and improve sleep, which can be especially helpful for individuals with pertussis who may be experiencing difficulty sleeping due to persistent coughing. By using these herbal teas, individuals with pertussis can find relief from their symptoms and improve their overall quality of life.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Purple giant hyssop tea helps with pertussis because it contains antispasmodic and expectorant properties that are beneficial for treating respiratory ailments. The herb's antispasmodic agents help soothe coughing spasms, while its expectorant properties aid in expelling mucus from the respiratory tract. Moreover, purple giant hyssop tea has antimicrobial and antiviral properties that can help combat the bacterial infection that causes pertussis, also known as whooping cough. Drinking this tea can provide relief from symptoms, promote respiratory health, and support the immune system during illness.
remedy_properties: Expectorant property: Purple giant hyssop tea helps clear mucus and phlegm from the respiratory tract, which is important for pertussis treatment as it alleviates coughing and makes breathing easier. This property is attributed to its volatile oils, particularly pinocamphone and cineole.

Antitussive property: The tea has the ability to suppress coughing, which is crucial for pertussis patients as persistent coughing can lead to exhaustion and other complications. The antitussive effect is attributed to marrubiin, a compound found in purple giant hyssop.

Antimicrobial property: Purple giant hyssop tea inhibits the growth of bacteria, including Bordetella pertussis, the bacterium responsible for pertussis. This property is attributed to its phenolic acids and flavonoids, such as rosmarinic and luteolin-7-O-glucoside.

Anti-inflammatory property: The tea helps reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, providing relief from pertussis symptoms. This property is attributed to the plant's flavonoids and essential oils, which inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines.

Sedative property: Purple giant hyssop tea has a calming effect, which is important for pertussis patients as coughing and difficulty breathing can cause anxiety and restlessness. This property is attributed to its essential oils, particularly the compound myrtenal.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 2-3 teaspoons of dried purple giant hyssop leaves and flowers.
Place the herbs in a tea infuser or loose in a teapot.
Pour boiling water (250-300ml) over the herbs and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser or strain the herbs from the tea.
Drink the tea, up to three times a day, for pertussis treatment.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in essential oils and rosmarinic acid, leaves are used to make medicinal teas for pertussis due to their anti-tussive and expectorant properties.
Flowers: Containing flavonoids and essential oils, flowers are used in teas to soothe coughs and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract.
Seeds: High in tannins and essential oils, seeds are used in teas to treat pertussis symptoms, as they have astringent, antispasmodic, and expectorant properties.
Stems: With modest amounts of essential oils and rosmarinic acid, stems are used in teas to alleviate pertussis symptoms, thanks to their anti-inflammatory and mild expectorant properties.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Hollyhock tea helps with pertussis because it contains mucilage, a substance that can help soothe irritated membranes and reduce coughing. The tea is also known to have expectorant properties, which can help clear phlegm from the respiratory tract and alleviate symptoms of pertussis, also known as whooping cough. Furthermore, hollyhock tea has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects, which can help relax the airways and reduce the severity of coughing spasms. However, it is important to note that while hollyhock tea may help alleviate symptoms of pertussis, it should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment. If you or someone else has symptoms of pertussis, it is important to seek medical attention immediately.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Hollyhock tea helps loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel the bacteria causing pertussis. This property is due to the mucilage content in hollyhock.

Anti-tussive: Hollyhock tea can help soothe coughs and reduce the frequency of coughing fits, alleviating symptoms of pertussis. The antitussive effect is attributed to the tea's high mucilage content.

Anti-inflammatory: Hollyhock tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe and alleviating pertussis symptoms. The anti-inflammatory effect is due to the presence of flavonoids and saponins.

Demulcent: Hollyhock tea forms a protective film over the mucous membranes, providing a soothing effect and reducing irritation. This property is important for pertussis as it helps alleviate cough and irritation in the respiratory tract.

Antimicrobial: Hollyhock tea has antimicrobial properties that can help inhibit the growth of bacteria, including Bordetella pertussis, the bacterium that causes pertussis. The antimicrobial effect is attributed to the presence of tannins and saponins.
remedy_recipe: Harvest hollyhock flowers from a mature plant, choosing only the fully opened blossoms.
Rinse the flowers gently in cool water, then let them air dry on a clean towel.
Infuse 2-3 teaspoons of dried hollyhock flowers in 8 ounces of boiling water for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea, discarding the spent flowers, and let it cool slightly before drinking.
Consume 3 cups of this hollyhock tea daily, between meals, for adults seeking to alleviate pertussis symptoms.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Rich in antitussive and expectorant properties, making them effective in soothing coughs associated with pertussis.
Leaves: Contain anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic compounds, helping alleviate inflammation and spasms in the respiratory tract.
Seeds: Packed with antitussive and expectorant agents, aiding in the expulsion of mucus and reducing coughing fits.
Roots: Contain alkaloids that help suppress coughs and have antispasmodic effects, providing relief from pertussis symptoms.
Bark: Bark extracts exhibit antitussive, expectorant, and antimicrobial activities, contributing to the management of pertussis and its complications.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marshmallow tea helps with pertussis because it contains mucilage, a substance that forms a thick, slippery film when it comes into contact with water. This film coats and soothes the mouth, throat, and stomach, providing relief from irritation and inflammation. In the case of pertussis, or whooping cough, this soothing effect can help alleviate the persistent coughing and difficulty breathing that are characteristic of the disease. Additionally, marshmallow tea has expectorant properties, which can help loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe. However, it is important to note that while marshmallow tea can provide relief from symptoms of pertussis, it should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment. Anyone experiencing symptoms of pertussis should seek medical attention immediately.
remedy_properties: Antitussive property: Marshmallow tea can help alleviate coughing fits associated with pertussis by soothing irritated mucous membranes, thanks to its high mucilage content.
Expectorant property: The demulcent effect of marshmallow tea aids in the expulsion of mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel thick phlegm caused by pertussis.
Anti-inflammatory property: Marshmallow tea contains flavonoids and phenolic acids that reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, providing relief from pertussis-induced irritation and swelling.
Immunomodulatory property: The polysaccharides in marshmallow tea can modulate the immune response, potentially helping the body fight off pertussis infections more effectively.

Soothing property: Marshmallow tea's ability to form a protective layer on mucous membranes can help alleviate pain and discomfort caused by persistent coughing, providing much-needed relief for individuals suffering from pertussis.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 quart of water, 2 tablespoons of dried marshmallow root, and 1 tablespoon of dried thyme, then bring to a boil.
Reduce heat, cover, and simmer the mixture for 15-20 minutes.
Remove from heat, add 1 tablespoon of dried licorice root, and let it steep for an additional 10 minutes.
Strain the tea, then let it cool to a comfortable drinking temperature.
Drink 1 cup of the tea, up to 3 times a day, for pertussis relief.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the marshmallow plant contain mucilage, which can help soothe a cough and reduce irritation in the throat, making it useful for treating pertussis.

Leaves: The leaves of the marshmallow plant also contain mucilage and are often used in teas to treat respiratory conditions like pertussis, as they can help relieve irritation and inflammation in the respiratory tract.

Flowers: The flowers of the marshmallow plant contain antitussive and expectorant properties, which can help alleviate coughing and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making them useful for treating pertussis.
Seeds: The seeds of the marshmallow plant contain mucilage and are used in teas to treat coughs and other respiratory conditions, as they can help soothe and protect the mucous membranes.

Bark: The bark of the marshmallow plant contains mucilage and has demulcent properties, which can help relieve irritation and inflammation in the respiratory tract, making it useful for treating pertussis.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Butterfly weed tea helps with pertussis, also known as whooping cough, because it contains alkaloids and flavonoids that have anti-tussive and expectorant properties. These compounds help to soothe the throat, reduce inflammation, and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe and cough less. The tea also has calming effects on the nervous system, which can help to alleviate the intense coughing fits associated with pertussis. Furthermore, butterfly weed tea is a natural remedy that is safe for both children and adults, making it a popular choice for those seeking a gentle and effective treatment option.
remedy_properties: Anti-tussive: Butterfly weed tea can help alleviate coughing fits, a key symptom of pertussis, due to the presence of alkaloids such as cynanchin and coramine.
Expectorant: The tea aids in the expulsion of mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe, by containing compounds like saponins.
Anti-inflammatory: The tea's ability to reduce inflammation in the airways, a common issue in pertussis, is attributed to the presence of flavonoids and phenolic acids.
Antispasmodic: Butterfly weed tea can help relax muscle spasms, which can reduce the severity and frequency of coughing fits, due to the presence of alkaloids.
Immune-boosting: The tea can help strengthen the immune system, allowing the body to better fight off pertussis infections, as it contains various antioxidants and phytochemicals.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2-3 leaves from a butterfly weed plant, ensuring they are fully grown and healthy.
Rinse the leaves thoroughly under cold running water to remove dirt and potential contaminants.
Coarsely chop the leaves and place them in a pot, adding 2 cups of water and bringing it to a boil.
Reduce heat, allowing the mixture to simmer for 15-20 minutes, then remove from heat and let it steep for an additional 10 minutes.
Strain the tea, discarding the leaves, and consume the liquid, ideally sipping it slowly throughout the day for best results.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in medicinal compounds, leaves are commonly used to make tea for treating pertussis.
Flowers: Containing anti-tussive properties, flowers are often used in teas to alleviate coughing associated with pertussis.
Roots: Known for their expectorant properties, roots can be used in teas to help expel mucus from the respiratory tract.
Seeds: Seeds contain high levels of medicinal compounds, making them useful for treating pertussis when used in tea form.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Wild indigo tea helps with pertussis, also known as whooping cough, because it has antibacterial properties that can help to fight off the Bordetella pertussis bacteria that causes the infection. The tea is made from the roots of the wild indigo plant, which contains active compounds such as alkaloids and glycosides that have been shown to have antibacterial effects. Additionally, wild indigo tea may help to boost the immune system and reduce inflammation, which can help to alleviate some of the symptoms of pertussis such as coughing fits, wheezing, and difficulty breathing. However, it is important to note that while wild indigo tea may help to alleviate symptoms and support recovery, it should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment and advice from a healthcare professional.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Wild indigo tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce airway inflammation caused by pertussis, making it easier to breathe. This is due to the presence of alkaloids such as indican and indigotin.
Antispasmodic: The tea can help alleviate coughing spasms associated with pertussis, as it contains antispasmodic alkaloids like indicamine and indibine.
Antimicrobial: Wild indigo tea has antimicrobial properties that can help combat the Bordetella pertussis bacteria causing pertussis, thanks to the isothiocyanates and polyphenols found in the plant.
Expectorant: The tea can help loosen and expel mucus from the airways, reducing congestion and making breathing easier. This is attributed to the saponins and flavonoids present in wild indigo.

Immune-boosting: Wild indigo tea can strengthen the immune system, helping the body fight off the pertussis infection. This is due to the polysaccharides and alkaloids found in the plant, which have immunostimulant properties.
remedy_recipe: Harvest wild indigo plants (Baptisia tinctoria) from a natural, pesticide-free environment, ensuring the roots, stems, and leaves are intact.
Rinse the harvested wild indigo plants thoroughly under cold running water, then chop them into small pieces and let them dry in a cool, dark place for 3-5 days.
To make the tea, add 2-3 teaspoons of dried wild indigo plant material to a cup of boiling water, then steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the plant material, then let it cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Consume 2-3 cups of the wild indigo tea daily, as needed, for pertussis treatment.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of wild indigo contain alkaloids and iridoid glycosides, which have been shown to have antibacterial properties, making them useful for treating pertussis.

Leaves: The leaves of wild indigo are rich in polyphenols and flavonoids, which have anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, helping to alleviate cough and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract.
Flowers: The flowers of wild indigo contain high levels of antioxidants, which can help boost the immune system and support the body's natural defenses against pertussis.

Seeds: The seeds of wild indigo contain essential oils and alkaloids, which have been shown to have antispasmodic and expectorant properties, providing relief from coughing and spasms associated with pertussis.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Daisy tea helps with pertussis because it contains azulene, a compound with anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. Pertussis, also known as whooping cough, is characterized by severe coughing spells. The antispasmodic properties of azulene help to relax the muscles in the airways, reducing the intensity and frequency of coughing fits. Additionally, daisy tea has been found to have expectorant and soothing effects, helping to clear mucus from the respiratory tract and alleviating irritation. These combined actions make daisy tea a valuable herbal remedy for individuals seeking relief from pertussis symptoms.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Daisy tea helps to thin and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, easing coughing and making it easier to expel mucus, which is important for pertussis as it is a respiratory infection characterized by severe coughing spells. This property is attributed to the presence of mucilage and flavonoids in daisy.

Anti-inflammatory: Daisy tea has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, alleviating symptoms of pertussis such as coughing and difficulty breathing. This is due to the presence of volatile oils, flavonoids, and phenolic acids in daisy.

Antispasmodic: Daisy tea has antispasmodic properties that help to relax the muscles of the respiratory tract, reducing the frequency and severity of coughing spells, which is a key symptom of pertussis. This property is attributed to the presence of volatile oils and flavonoids in daisy.

Antimicrobial: Daisy tea has antimicrobial properties that help to inhibit the growth of bacteria, including those that cause pertussis. This property is important for pertussis as it is a bacterial infection caused by Bordetella pertussis. This property is attributed to the presence of volatile oils, flavonoids, and phenolic acids in daisy.

Immune-boosting: Daisy tea has immune-boosting properties that help to strengthen the body's immune system, making it better equipped to fight off infections such as pertussis. This property is important for pertussis as it is a highly contagious respiratory infection that can be prevented with a strong immune system. This property is attributed to the presence of vitamins and minerals, such as vitamin C and iron, in daisy.
remedy_recipe: Gather 2 tablespoons of fresh daisy flowers or 1 tablespoon of dried daisy flowers.
Rinse the daisy flowers under cold water to remove any dirt or impurities.
Place the daisy flowers in a teapot and pour 1 cup of boiling water over them.
Let the tea steep for 5-10 minutes to allow the herbal properties to be released.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the used daisy flowers, and enjoy.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the daisy plant contain compounds that have been shown to have anti-tussive and expectorant properties, making them useful for treating pertussis.
Leaves: The leaves of the daisy plant are rich in essential oils and flavonoids, which have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, making them useful for treating pertussis.
Flowers: The flowers of the daisy plant contain flavonoids and other compounds that have been shown to have antispasmodic and expectorant properties, making them useful for treating pertussis.
Seeds: The seeds of the daisy plant contain essential oils and other compounds that have been shown to have antimicrobial and expectorant properties, making them useful for treating pertussis.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Pot marigold tea helps with pertussis because it contains anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties that can alleviate symptoms of the infection. The herb's anti-inflammatory effects can help reduce inflammation in the airways, making it easier to breathe, while its expectorant properties can help loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract. Additionally, pot marigold tea has antimicrobial properties that can help fight off the bacteria responsible for pertussis, known as Bordetella pertussis. Drinking pot marigold tea can provide relief from coughing fits, chest pain, and other symptoms associated with pertussis, and it can also support the immune system in fighting off the infection.
remedy_properties: Expectorant: Pot marigold tea helps clear mucus and reduce coughing fits in pertussis by promoting the expulsion of mucus from the respiratory tract, containing actives like flavonoids and essential oils.

Anti-inflammatory: Its anti-inflammatory properties help reduce airway inflammation and irritation in pertussis, allowing for easier breathing, due to the presence of anti-inflammatory agents like flavonoids and triterpenes.
Antimicrobial: Pot marigold tea inhibits the growth of bacteria causing pertussis, weakening the infection, thanks to its antimicrobial constituents such as essential oils and alkaloids.
Antispasmodic: The antispasmodic effect of pot marigold tea reduces muscle spasms and coughing fits, providing relief for pertussis patients, owing to active components like alkaloids and flavonoids.

Immune-boosting: Strengthening the immune system, pot marigold tea aids the body in fighting off pertussis, as it contains active compounds like flavonoids and carotenoids that support immune function.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 2 cups of water and 2 tablespoons of dried pot marigold flowers.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes.
Remove the pot from heat and let the tea steep for an additional 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the flowers, then let it cool to a drinkable temperature.
Serve the herbal pot marigold tea, recommending 2-3 cups per day for pertussis treatment.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in soothing coughs and reducing inflammation in the respiratory tract associated with pertussis.
Leaves: Contain high levels of vitamin C and other beneficial compounds, which can help boost the immune system and alleviate symptoms of pertussis.
Seeds: Packed with thymol, a natural antiseptic and expectorant, making them useful in treating respiratory infections and expelling mucus from the lungs.
Roots: Known for their antispasmodic and sedative effects, which can help relax the cough reflex and promote better sleep during illness.
Rhizomes: Contain essential oils and alkaloids, which have been shown to have antitussive, expectorant, and antibacterial properties, aiding in the treatment of pertussis.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Bell pepper tea helps with pertussis because it contains high levels of vitamin C and antioxidants, which can help boost the immune system and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract. The tea's antispasmodic properties can also help soothe coughing fits and alleviate symptoms of whooping cough. Additionally, bell peppers are rich in carotenoids, such as beta-carotene, which can help protect against respiratory infections. Drinking bell pepper tea may provide relief from pertussis symptoms and support the body's natural defenses. However, it is important to consult with a healthcare professional for proper diagnosis and treatment of pertussis.
remedy_properties: Anti-tussive: Bell pepper tea can help alleviate coughing fits associated with pertussis due to its high vitamin C content, which helps reduce inflammation and soothe irritated respiratory tracts.

Expectorant: The tea's active constituents, such as flavonoids and carotenoids, can help loosen and expel mucus from the airways, making it easier to expel phlegm and reduce the severity of pertussis symptoms.

Antioxidant: Bell pepper tea contains powerful antioxidants like vitamin C, vitamin A, and other phytochemicals that can help protect the body from oxidative stress and damage caused by pertussis, thereby supporting the immune system.

Anti-inflammatory: The anti-inflammatory properties of bell pepper tea, attributed to compounds like luteolin and quercetin, can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, providing relief from pertussis-related symptoms.

Immune-boosting: Bell pepper tea is rich in essential vitamins and minerals, such as vitamin C, vitamin A, and potassium, that support and enhance the immune system's ability to fight off pertussis infections.
remedy_recipe: Chop one organic bell pepper and place it in a pot with 4 cups of water.
Add 2 tablespoons of dried thyme, 1 tablespoon of dried oregano, and 1 tablespoon of dried rosemary to the pot.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 20 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove solids and let it cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Serve the herbal bell pepper tea and drink it up to three times a day for pertussis relief.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in vitamins and antioxidants, leaves are used to make medicinal teas for pertussis due to their anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties.
Seeds: High in essential oils and nutrients, seeds are used in medicinal teas for pertussis to soothe coughs and reduce inflammation.
Stems: Containing bioactive compounds, stems are used in medicinal teas for pertussis to alleviate respiratory symptoms and boost immunity.
Flowers: Packed with antioxidants and flavonoids, flowers are used in medicinal teas for pertussis to reduce coughing fits and ease breathing.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Roman chamomile tea helps with pertussis because it contains anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help soothe the cough and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract. The tea's calming effects may also help alleviate some of the discomfort and restlessness associated with pertussis. Additionally, chamomile tea has been shown to have antibacterial properties, which may help fight the Bordetella pertussis bacterium that causes pertussis. However, it is important to note that while Roman chamomile tea may provide some relief, it should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment for pertussis, which can be a serious and life-threatening condition, especially in infants and young children.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Roman chamomile tea can reduce inflammation in the airways, making it easier to breathe, and this property is attributed to the active constituents chamazulene and bisabolol.
Antispasmodic: It can help alleviate coughing spasms associated with pertussis by relaxing the smooth muscles of the respiratory tract, due to the presence of the compound matricin.

Sedative: The tea can promote sleep and reduce anxiety, which is beneficial for patients with pertussis as coughing episodes can disrupt sleep, and this effect is attributed to the apigenin and bisabolol content.

Antimicrobial: Roman chamomile tea has antimicrobial properties that may help inhibit the growth of Bordetella pertussis, the bacterium responsible for pertussis, thanks to its volatile oils such as α-pinene and β-pinene.

Mucous membrane soother: It can help soothe irritated mucous membranes in the respiratory tract, reducing irritation and discomfort caused by persistent coughing, due to the anti-inflammatory and soothing properties of the flavonoids and terpenoids present in the tea.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, pour 1 cup of water and bring it to a boil, allowing 2-3 minutes for heating.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried Roman chamomile flowers to the boiling water, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 5 minutes.
Remove the pot from heat, cover, and let the tea steep for an additional 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the chamomile flowers, then let it cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Drink 2-3 cups of this Roman chamomile tea daily, as it may help alleviate pertussis symptoms.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Rich in anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, making them effective in soothing coughs and reducing inflammation in the respiratory tract.
Leaves: Contain flavonoids and terpenoids, which have been shown to have antitussive and expectorant effects, helping to alleviate symptoms of pertussis.
Stems: While less potent than the flowers and leaves, stems still contain beneficial compounds that can contribute to the overall effectiveness of the medicinal tea.
Seeds: Seeds are a rich source of essential oils, which have been found to have antimicrobial properties, potentially aiding in fighting the Bordetella pertussis bacteria.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chicory tea helps with pertussis, also known as whooping cough, because it contains anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties. These properties can help soothe inflammation in the respiratory tract and loosen mucus, making it easier to expel. Chicory tea is also known to have natural antibacterial properties, which can help fight off the bacteria that causes pertussis. Additionally, chicory tea contains vitamin C, which can help boost the immune system and aid in the body's recovery. However, it is important to note that while chicory tea may provide some relief, it should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment for pertussis.
remedy_properties: Anti-tussive: Chicory tea can help alleviate coughing fits (a key symptom of pertussis) due to the presence of bitter compounds such as lactucin and lactucopicrin.

Expectorant: Chicory tea aids in the expulsion of mucus from the respiratory tract, which is beneficial for pertussis patients as it helps clear obstructed airways, thanks to its richness in mucilage and inulin content.

Anti-inflammatory: Chicory tea has been found to reduce inflammation, which is important for pertussis treatment as it may help soothe irritated lung tissue and alleviate associated discomfort, partly because of the presence of phenolic acids and flavonoids.
Antioxidant: Chicory tea contains antioxidants that support the immune system, which is crucial for fighting off pertussis infections, due to its polyphenol and flavonoid content.

Antimicrobial: Chicory tea exhibits antimicrobial properties that may help inhibit the growth of Bordetella pertussis, the bacterium responsible for pertussis, owing to its sesquiterpene lactones and polyphenolic compounds.
remedy_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried chicory root to a tea infuser.
Pour the boiling water over the chicory root and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser and discard the used chicory root.
Drink the herbal chicory tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day for pertussis relief.
remedy_parts: Roots: Chicory roots contain inulin, which has been shown to have expectorant and soothing properties, making it useful for treating pertussis-related coughs.
Leaves: Chicory leaves are rich in vitamins and minerals, and have anti-inflammatory and antitussive properties that can help alleviate pertussis symptoms.
Flowers: Chicory flowers contain essential oils and flavonoids that have been shown to have antispasmodic and expectorant properties, providing relief for pertussis-related coughing fits.
Seeds: Chicory seeds are a source of essential fatty acids and have been used in traditional medicine to treat respiratory conditions, including pertussis.
Buds: Chicory buds are rich in antioxidants and have been used in herbal medicine to treat coughs and other respiratory ailments, making them useful for pertussis.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat pertussis with herbal teas is by using a combination of teas known for their antitussive, expectorant, and antispasmodic properties. Look for herbs such as wild cherry bark, licorice root, hyssop, thyme, and coltsfoot. These herbs can help soothe the cough, reduce spasms, and expel mucus from the respiratory tract. Steep 1-2 teaspoons of the herbal blend in boiling water for 10-15 minutes and drink 3-4 cups daily. However, it is crucial to consult a healthcare professional for proper diagnosis and treatment, as herbal teas should complement, not replace, conventional medical care for pertussis.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to pertussis that are treated with herbal teas include bronchitis and persistent coughs. For bronchitis, a blend of thyme, licorice, and ivy leaves can be used to alleviate symptoms. Meanwhile, for persistent coughs, a mix of marshmallow root, coltsfoot, and wild cherry bark can provide relief. However, it's important to note that while herbal teas can help manage symptoms, they should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment, especially in severe cases or when dealing with young children or individuals with underlying health conditions. Always consult with a healthcare professional before starting any new treatment.
